contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Binary-search a predicate only when its truth pattern has one directional transition over the ordered domain; scan arbitrary conditions that can change back and forth.

items.prompt: Which truth pattern supports a first-true binary search over increasing candidates?

options.text: false, false, ..., false, true, true, ..., true.
false, true, false, true.
false, false, true, false, false.
Any pattern, provided mid is checked.

feedback.reason: A first-true search needs one persistent false-to-true transition.
feedback.details: A false midpoint eliminates the prefix; a true midpoint retains the possibility of an earlier transition.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.alternating: Multiple transitions prevent one directional discard.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_true: Truth does not remain true after the transition.

items.prompt: The condition P(i) is true when the sorted value at index i is at least target. Why is P monotonic?

options.text: Once one value is at least target, every later sorted value is also at least target.
Indices increase numerically.
A midpoint can be computed.
The target is fixed.

feedback.reason: Monotonicity is an implication across the ordered domain.
feedback.details: The sorted-value contract extends one true comparison to every later index.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_increases: Index order alone does not constrain the condition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_exists: Every finite index range has a midpoint, including nonmonotonic conditions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_fixed: A fixed target does not make arbitrary input order monotonic.

items.prompt: The condition P(i) is true when the sorted value at index i is even. Which method is generally required to find the first even value?

options.text: A scan, because parity can alternate throughout sorted numeric values.
Binary search, because the values are sorted.
A first-true template without a monotonicity proof.
Lower bound for the value 2.

feedback.reason: An arbitrary property of sorted values need not be monotonic in the sort key.
feedback.details: A preprocessed index of even positions could change the representation, but the raw sorted array does not support half elimination for parity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary: Sorted numeric magnitude does not order parity truth.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true: The predicate can revert from true to false.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_bound_two: The first even value need not be 2 and later odd values remain possible.

items.prompt: A candidate capacity x is feasible if all jobs finish before a deadline, and increasing capacity cannot make the schedule worse. Which strategy contrast is correct?

options.text: Binary-search the first feasible capacity; a scan is correct but may inspect many more candidates.
Only a scan is correct because feasibility is computed rather than stored.
Binary search is valid for every computed boolean predicate.
Search for a capacity value already present in the job list.

feedback.reason: A monotonic feasibility predicate creates an ordered answer boundary.
feedback.details: The binary search is legal even though the candidates are not array elements; the scan remains a correct but potentially slower baseline.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_only: A computed predicate can still be monotonic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_search: The answer domain is capacity values, not input membership.

items.prompt: A condition over candidates is true for x < 10, false for 10 <= x < 20, and true again for x >= 20. Can ordinary binary search find the first true value without additional structure?

options.text: No. The true set has multiple separated regions, so one midpoint outcome cannot eliminate a whole side consistently.
Yes. A first true exists at the beginning.
Yes. Ignore the earlier true region and search the later one.
Yes, with an upper-biased midpoint.

feedback.reason: Ordinary boundary search represents one transition, not multiple valid intervals.
feedback.details: A scan or interval-specific decomposition is needed unless the query is reformulated.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_first_true: The template assumes a false prefix followed by a true suffix, which is not present.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_last_region: That changes the requested first-true contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_upper_mid: Bias affects progress, not predicate monotonicity.

items.prompt: Which statement is sufficient to prove a false-to-true predicate P is monotonic?

options.text: For all a <= b, P(a) true implies P(b) true.
P is true at one large candidate.
P is false at one small candidate.
The data used by P is sorted.

feedback.reason: Monotonicity is a universal implication over candidate order.
feedback.details: The equivalent contrapositive says P(b) false implies every earlier a is also false.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_true_example: One observation does not constrain other candidates.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_false_example: A single false does not prove a false prefix.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_input: P may depend on another nonmonotonic property.

items.prompt: A predicate is monotonic, but it changes from true to false as candidates increase. Which boundary search matches a maximum feasible answer?

options.text: Search for the last true candidate.
Search for the first true candidate.
Return the first false candidate.
Monotonic true-to-false predicates cannot be binary-searched.

feedback.reason: The transition direction and optimization objective determine the retained side.
feedback.details: Monotonicity supports binary search in either direction when the boundary contract is derived correctly.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_true: That usually returns the smallest, not the maximum feasible value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_required: They support last-true or first-false search.

items.prompt: A predicate is deterministic but nonmonotonic. Does determinism alone justify binary search?

options.text: No. Stable repeated answers are necessary but not sufficient; one directional transition is still required.
Yes. Deterministic booleans always form a searchable boundary.
Yes whenever candidate values are ordered.
Yes whenever P(x) is O(1).

feedback.reason: Predicate stability and predicate monotonicity are distinct requirements.
feedback.details: A scan can exploit deterministic evaluation without assuming a false or true region.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes: They can alternate in a fixed repeatable pattern.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_sorted_domain: The truth relation must align with that order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_constant_cost: Cheap evaluation does not create a discard implication.

items.prompt: A sorted array is queried for the first value whose decimal representation contains digit 7. Which diagnosis is correct?

options.text: The condition is arbitrary in numeric order, so scan unless a separate index or interval decomposition is provided.
Binary-search because the numeric values are sorted.
Use lower_bound(7) and return that index.
Check the midpoint digit pattern and discard the opposite pattern.

feedback.reason: The query property must be monotonic in the sort order, not merely definable from each value.
feedback.details: A specialized index of values containing digit 7 could change the strategy boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_binary: Digit containment can appear and disappear repeatedly.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_bound_7: Values such as 17, 70, and 107 do not form one suffix beginning at 7.

items.prompt: A first-true predicate is false for all candidates. Which result distinguishes a boundary search from an exact-match search?

options.text: Return the declared upper sentinel or not-found boundary, such as n for [0, n).
Return the last midpoint checked.
Return the first candidate even though it is false.
Return the first candidate a scan would visit.

feedback.reason: Monotonic boundary search remains defined even when the transition lies just outside the concrete domain.
feedback.details: The sentinel behavior must be part of the contract before choosing binary search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_mid: It is false and has no boundary meaning.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_first: Visit order does not define the first true boundary.

items.prompt: Which predicates are monotonic in the stated increasing candidate direction? Select all that apply.

options.text: In ascending data, valueAt(i) >= target.
Capacity x is sufficient when larger capacity cannot hurt.
A demanded minimum distance d is achievable when larger d can only make placement harder.
Candidate x is prime.

feedback.reason: Monotonicity can be false-to-true or true-to-false.
feedback.details: The key is a one-directional implication, not a particular boolean orientation.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.at_least_target: Once true, later values remain at least target.

items.prompt: Which conditions are arbitrary rather than single-boundary predicates in ordinary numeric order? Select all that apply.

options.text: x is even.
The decimal representation of x contains digit 7.
x equals one fixed target across all integers.
x is at least target.

feedback.reason: An exact equality spike or recurring property is not a persistent prefix or suffix.
feedback.details: Exact binary search becomes legal only when x indexes sorted stored values and comparisons supply a different regional implication.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.contains_digit: Truth can appear and disappear repeatedly.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.equals_target: Truth is false, true once, then false.

items.prompt: Which facts should be checked before replacing a predicate scan with binary search? Select all that apply.

options.text: Candidates have a declared total order and bounded search range.
The predicate returns a stable result for the same candidate during the search.
A proof establishes one directional truth transition.
The helper returns a boolean.

feedback.reason: A boolean interface does not imply a binary-searchable boolean structure.
feedback.details: The scan can remain correct without the transition proof; binary search cannot.

items.prompt: Which edge cases belong to a valid first-true monotonic contract? Select all that apply.

options.text: The predicate is false for the entire domain.
The predicate is true for the entire domain.
The transition occurs at the first or last concrete candidate.
The predicate changes false-to-true and later true-to-false.

feedback.reason: A monotonic predicate may have no internal transition or one at an endpoint.
feedback.details: Multiple transitions are not an edge case of the same algorithm; they change the problem structure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_false: The boundary may be the upper sentinel.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_true: The first candidate is the boundary.

items.prompt: Which changes can convert an arbitrary-condition scan into a legal binary-search workflow? Select all that apply.

options.text: Build and maintain a sorted index of candidates satisfying the condition, then search that index for an aligned query.
Reformulate the objective as a monotonic threshold predicate and prove it.
Decompose the domain into known monotonic intervals and search the relevant interval under an explicit rule.
Rename the predicate to isValid.

feedback.reason: The strategy boundary moves when new structure is created or proved.
feedback.details: Cosmetic changes do not turn an arbitrary predicate into a monotonic one.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rename_condition: A name does not change its truth pattern.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.precompute_sorted_matches: The representation now has ordered searchable structure.

items.prompt: Arrange the audit for deciding whether a predicate scan can become binary search.

elements.text: Look for a < b < c that creates more than one truth transition.
If no counterexample exists, prove the directional implication for all candidates.
Use boundary search when proven monotonic; otherwise retain scanning or add structure.

feedback.reason: Counterexample search is a diagnostic aid, while correctness ultimately needs a universal proof.
feedback.details: The final strategy follows the predicate structure rather than the boolean syntax.

items.prompt: A monotonic predicate over W candidates costs O(1) per evaluation. An arbitrary-condition scan over the same W candidates also costs O(1) per evaluation. Select worst-case time for each.
constraints: The monotonic search halves the candidate range.
The arbitrary condition may require checking every candidate.

feedback.reason: The asymptotic contrast follows from predicate structure.
feedback.details: Without monotonicity, midpoint evaluation cannot reduce the number of required candidate checks logarithmically.

items.prompt: A monotonic predicate evaluation costs O(n), and its candidate range has W values. A direct scan over W candidates evaluates the same O(n) condition at each candidate. Select worst-case time for both.
constraints: Binary search performs O(log W) predicate calls.
The scan may perform W predicate calls.

feedback.reason: Binary search reduces the number of expensive evaluations only because the predicate is monotonic.
feedback.details: The full predicate cost remains part of both totals.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Binary-search a predicate only when its truth pattern has one directional transition over the ordered domain; scan arbitrary conditions that can change back and forth.
